author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

ccel.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

title: Homily X

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Commendation of those who came to hear after taking a meal.—Observations on the physiology of the natural world; and against those who deify the creation; and on the duty of not swearing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I joy, and rejoice with you all, that ye have actually put in practice that admonition of ours, which we lately made with respect to those who were absent, for the reason that they were not fasting.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I think that many of those who have dined are to-day present; and go to fill up this goodly assemblage; and that this is the fact, I conjecture from the more brilliant spectacle that I see around me, and the greater concourse of hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Not in vain, it seems, did I lately spend so many words on their account, appealing to your Charity, to draw them to their Mother; and to persuade them that it is lawful, even after bodily nourishment, to partake also of that which is spiritual.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And in which case, beloved, I ask, did ye act for the better; at the time of the last assembly when after your meal ye turned to your slumbers; or now, when after the meal ye have presented yourselves at the hearing of the divine laws?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Was it best when ye loitered about in the forum, and took part in meetings which were no wise profitable; or now, when ye stand with your own brethren, and hear the prophetic oracles?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is no disgrace, beloved, to have eaten, but after eating to remain at home, and so to be deprived of this sacred banquet.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For whilst thou remainest at home, thou wilt be more slothful and supine; but coming here thou wilt shake off all slumber and listlessness; and laying aside not only listlessness, but also all sadness, thou wilt be more at ease, and in better heart in all the events that may happen.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What need then is there to say more?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Stand only nigh the man who fasts, and thou wilt straightway partake of his good odour; for fasting is a spiritual perfume; and through the eyes, the tongue, and every part, it manifests the good disposition of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I have said this, not for the purpose of condemning those who have dined, but that I may shew the advantage of fasting.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I do not, however, call mere abstinence from meats, fasting; but even before this, abstinence from sin; since he who, after he has taken a meal, has come hither with suitable sobriety, is not very far behind the man who fasts; even as he who continues fasting, if he does not give earnest and diligent heed to what is spoken, will derive no great benefit from his fast.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He who eats, and yet takes a part in the sacred assembly with suitable earnestness, is in much better case than he who eats not at all, and remains absent.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This abstinence will by no means be able to benefit us as much as the participation in spiritual instruction conveyeth to us benefit and advantage.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Where indeed, besides, wilt thou hear the things upon which thou meditatest here?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Wert thou to go to the bench of justice? quarrels and contentions are there! or into the council-chamber? there is anxious thought about political matters! or to thine home? solicitude on the subject of thy private affairs afflicts thee in every direction! or wert thou to go to the conferences and debates of the forum? every thing there is earthly and corruptible!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For all the words that pass among those assembled there, are concerning merchandize, or taxes, or the sumptuous table, or the sale of lands, or other contracts, or wills, or inheritances, or some other things of that kind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And shouldest thou enter even into the royal halls, there again thou wouldest hear in the same way all discoursing of wealth, or power, or of the glory which is held in honour here, but of nothing that is spiritual.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But here on the contrary everything relates to heaven, and heavenly things; to our soul, to our life, the purpose for which we were born, and why we spend an allotted time upon earth, and on what terms we migrate from hence, and into what condition we shall enter after these things, and why our body is of clay, what also is the nature of death, what, in short, the present life is, and what the future.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The discourses that are here made by us contain nothing at all of an earthly kind, but are all in reference to spiritual things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus, then, it is that we shall have made great provision for our salvation, and shall depart hence with a good hope.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Since, therefore, we did not scatter the seed in vain, but ye hunted out all who were absent, as I exhorted you; suffer us now to return you a recompense; and having reminded you of a few things that were said before, to repay you again what remains.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then were those matters that were before treated of?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We were enquiring how, and in what manner, before the giving of the Scriptures, God ordered His dispensation toward us; and we said, that by means of the creation He instructed our race, stretching out the heavens, and there openly unfolding a vast volume, useful alike to the simple and the wise, to the poor and to the rich, to Scythians and to barbarians, and to all in general who dwell upon the earth; a volume which is much larger than the multitude of those instructed by it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We discoursed also at length concerning the night, and the day, and the order of these, as well as of the harmony which is strictly preserved by them; and much was said respecting the measured dance of the seasons of the year, and of their equality.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For just as the day defraudeth not the night even of half an hour throughout the whole year, so also do these distribute all the days among themselves equally.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But, as I said before, not only does the greatness and beauty of the creation shew forth the Divine Architect, but the very manner likewise in which it is compacted together, and the method of operation, transcending as it does, the ordinary course of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it would have been in accordance with nature for water to be borne upon the earth; but now we see, on the contrary, that the earth is supported by the waters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It would have been in accordance with nature that fire should tend upwards; but now on the contrary we see the beams of the sun directed towards the earth; and the waters to be above the heavens, yet not falling away; and the sun running below them, yet not quenched by the waters, nor dispelling their moisture.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Besides these things we said that this whole universe consists of four elements, these being adverse to and at strife with one another; yet one does not consume the other, although they are mutually destructive.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whence it is evident that some invisible power bridles them, and the will of God becomes their bond.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: To-day, I wish to dwell a little more on this subject.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Arouse yourselves, however, and give earnest heed unto us!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And that the wonder may appear more clearly, I will draw the lesson concerning these things from our own bodies.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This body of ours, so short, and small, consists of four elements; viz. of what is warm, that is, of blood; of what is dry, that is, of yellow bile; of what is moist, that is, of phlegm; of what is cold, that is, of black bile.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And let no one think this subject foreign to that which we have in hand.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “For He that is spiritual judgeth all things; yet He Himself is judged of no man.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus also Paul touched upon principles of agriculture, whilst discoursing to us of the Resurrection; and said, “Thou fool; that which thou sowest is not quickened, except it die.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if that blessed man brought forward questions of agriculture, neither should any one blame us if we handle matters pertaining to medical science.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For our discourse is now respecting the Creation of God; and this ground-work of ideas will be necessary for our purpose.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As, therefore, I said before, this body of ours consists of four elements; and if either revolts against the whole, death is the result of this revolt.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As for instance, by a superabundance “of bile” fever is produced; and should this proceed beyond a certain measure, it effects a rapid dissolution.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Again, when there is an excess of the cold element, paralyses, agues, apoplexies, and an infinite number of other maladies are generated.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And every form of disease is the effect of an excess of these elements; when either of them overpassing its own bounds, acts the part of a tyrant against the rest, and mars the symmetry of the whole.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Interrogate then him who says, that all things are spontaneous and self-produced.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If this little and diminutive body, having the advantage of medicines, and of medical skill, and of a soul within which regulates it, and of much moral wisdom, as well as innumerable other helps, be not always able to continue in a state of order, but often perishes, and is destroyed, when some disturbance takes place within it; how could a world like this, containing substances of such vast bulk and compounded of those same elements, remain during so long a time without any disturbance, unless it enjoyed the advantage of a manifold providence?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Neither would it be reasonable to suppose that this body, which has the benefit of superintendence both without and within, should scarcely be sufficient for its own preservation; and that a world such as this is, enjoying no such superintendence, should during so many years suffer nothing of that sort which our body suffers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For how, I ask, is it that not one of these elements hath gone beyond its own boundaries, nor swallowed up all the rest?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who hath brought them together from the beginning?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who hath bound?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who hath bridled?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who hath held them together during so long a period?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if the body of the world were simple and uniform, what I speak of would not have been so impossible.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when there hath been such a strife between the elements, even from the beginning; who so senseless as to think that these things would have come together, and remained together when united, without One to effect this conjunction?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if we who are evil-affected towards one another not by nature, but by will, cannot come spontaneously to an agreement as long as we remain at variance, and hold ourselves ungraciously towards one another; if we have yet need of some one else to bring us into a state of conjunction; and after this conjunction further to clench us, and persuade us to abide by our reconciliation, and not again to be at variance; how could the elements, which neither partake of sense nor reason, and which are naturally adverse, and inimical to each other, have come together, and agreed and remained with one another, if there were not some ineffable Power which effected this conjunction; and after this conjunction, always restrained them by the same bond?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Dost thou not perceive how this body wastes away, withers, and perishes after the secession of the soul, and each of the elements thereof returns to its own appointed place?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This very same thing, indeed, would also happen to the world, if the Power which always governs it had left it devoid of Its own providence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if a ship does not hold together without a pilot, but soon founders, how could the world have held together so long a time if there was no one governing its course?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And that I may not enlarge, suppose the world to be a ship; the earth to be placed below as the keel; the sky to be the sail; men to be the passengers; the subjacent abyss, the sea.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How is it then that during so long a time, no shipwreck has taken place?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Now let a ship go one day without a pilot and crew, and thou wilt see it straightway foundering!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the world, though subsisting now five thousand years, and many more, hath suffered nothing of the kind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But why do I talk of a ship?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Suppose one hath pitched a small hut in the vineyards; and when the fruit is gathered, leaves it vacant; it stands, however, scarce two or three days, but soon goes to pieces, and tumbles down!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Could not a hut, forsooth, stand without superintendence?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How then could the workmanship of a world, so fair and marvellous; the laws of the night and day; the interchanging dances of the seasons; the course of nature chequered and varied as it is in every way throughout the earth, the sea, the sky; in plants, and in animals that fly, swim, walk, creep; and in the race of men, far more dignified than any of these, continue yet unbroken, during so long a period, without some kind of providence?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But in addition to what has been said, follow me whilst I enumerate the meadows, the gardens, the various tribes of flowers; all sorts of herbs, and their uses; their odours, forms, disposition, yea, but their very names; the trees which are fruitful, and which are barren; the nature of metals,—and of animals,—in the sea, or on the land; of those that swim, and those that traverse the air; the mountains, the forests, the groves; the meadow below, and the meadow above; for there is a meadow on the earth, and a meadow too in the sky; the various flowers of the stars; the rose below, and the rainbow above!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Would you have me point out also the meadow of birds?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Consider the variegated body of the peacock, surpassing every dye, and the fowls of purple plumage.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Contemplate with me the beauty of the sky; how it has been preserved so long without being dimmed; and remains as bright and clear as if it had been only fabricated to-day; moreover, the power of the earth, how its womb has not become effete by bringing forth during so long a time!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Contemplate with me the fountains; how they burst forth and fail not, since the time they were begotten, to flow forth continually throughout the day and night!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Contemplate with me the sea, receiving so many rivers, yet never exceeding its measure!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But how long shall we pursue things unattainable!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is fit, indeed, that over every one of these which has been spoken of, we should say, “O Lord, how hast Thou magnified Thy works; in wisdom hast Thou made them all.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what is the sapient argument of the unbelievers, when we go over all these particulars with them; the magnitude, the beauty of the creation, the prodigality, the munificence everywhere displayed?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This very thing, say they, is the worst fault, that God hath made the world so beautiful and so vast.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if He had not made it beautiful and vast, we should not have made a god of it; but now being struck with its grandeur, and marvelling at its beauty, we have thought it to be a deity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But such an argument is good for nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For that neither the magnitude, nor beauty of the world is the cause of this impiety, but their own want of understanding, is what we are prepared to show, proved by the case of ourselves, who have never been so affected.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Why then have “we” not made a deity of it?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do we not see it with the same eyes as themselves?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do we not enjoy the same advantage from the creation with themselves?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do we not possess the same soul?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Have we not the same body?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do we not tread the same earth?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How comes it that this beauty and magnitude hath not persuaded us to think the same as they do?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But this will be evident not from this proof only, but from another besides.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For as a proof that it is not for its beauty they have made a deity of it, but by reason of their own folly, why do they adore the ape, the crocodile, the dog, and the vilest of animals?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Truly, “they became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was darkened.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless, we will not frame our answer from these things only, but will also say something yet further.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God, foreseeing these things of old, destroyed, in His wisdom, this plea of theirs.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On this account He made the world not only wonderful and vast, but also corruptible and perishable; and placed therein many evidences of its weakness; and what He did with respect to the Apostles, He did with respect to the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then did He with respect to the Apostles?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Since they used to perform many great and astonishing signs and wonders, He suffered them constantly to be scourged, to be expelled, to inhabit the dungeon, to encounter bodily infirmities, to be in continual tribulations, lest the greatness of their miracles should make them to be accounted as gods amongst mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore when He had bestowed so great favour upon them, He suffered their bodies to be mortal, and in many cases obnoxious to disease; and did not remove their infirmity, that He might give full proof of their nature.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this is not merely my assertion, but that of Paul himself, who says, “For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool; but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, “But we have this treasure in earthen vessels.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what is meant by “earthen vessels?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In this body, he means, which is mortal and perishable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For just as the earthen vessel is formed from clay and fire, so also the body of these saints being clay, and receiving the energy of the spiritual fire, becomes an earthen vessel.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But for what reason was it thus constituted, and so great a treasure, and such a plentitude of graces entrusted to a mortal and corruptible body?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “That the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when thou seest the Apostles raising the dead, yet themselves sick, and unable to remove their own infirmities, thou mayest clearly perceive, that the resurrection of the dead man was not effected by the power of him who raised him, but by the energy of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For in proof, that they were frequently sick, hear what Paul saith respecting Timothy, “Use a little wine for thy stomach’s sake, and thine often infirmities.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, of another he saith, “But Trophimus I have left at Miletus sick.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And writing to the Philippians, he said, “Epaphroditus was sick nigh unto death.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if, when this was the case, they accounted them to be gods, and prepared to do sacrifice unto them, saying, “The gods are come down to us in the likeness of men;” had such infirmities not existed, to what extent of impiety might not men have proceeded, when they beheld their miracles?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As then in this case, because of the greatness of these signs, He suffered their nature to remain in a state of infirmity, and permitted those repeated trials, in order that they might not be thought to be gods, thus likewise He did with respect to the creation, a thing nearly parallel to this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For He fashioned it beautiful and vast; but on the other hand corruptible.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And both of these points the Scriptures teach, for one in treating of the beauty of the heavens thus speaks; “The heavens declare the glory of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, “Who hath placed the sky as a vault, and spread it out as a tent over the earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, “Who holdeth the circle of heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But another writer, shewing that although the world be great and fair, it is yet corruptible, thus speaks; “Thou, Lord, in the beginning hast laid the foundation of the earth, and the heavens are the works of Thine hands.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They shall perish, but Thou remainest, and they all shall wax old as doth a garment, and as a vesture shalt Thou fold them up, and they shall be changed.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, David saith of the sun, that “he is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, and rejoiceth as a giant to run his course.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou how he places before thee the beauty of this star, and its greatness?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For even as a bridegroom when he appears from some stately chamber, so the sun sends forth his rays under the East; and adorning the heaven as it were with a saffron-coloured veil, and making the clouds like roses, and running unimpeded all the day; he meets no obstacle to interrupt his course.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Beholdest thou, then, his beauty?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Beholdest thou his greatness?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Look also at the proof of his weakness!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For a certain wise man, to make this plain, said, “What is brighter than the sun, yet the light thereof suffers eclipse.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nor is it only from this circumstance that his infirmity is to be perceived, but also in the concourse of the clouds.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Often, at least, when a cloud passes underneath him, though emitting his beams, and endeavouring to pierce through it, he has not strength to do so; the cloud being too dense, and not suffering him to penetrate through it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “He nourishes the seeds, however,” replies some one—Yes—still he does not nourish them by himself, but requires the assistance of the earth, and of the dew, and of the rains, and of the winds, and the right distribution of the seasons.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And unless all these things concur, the sun’s aid is but superfluous.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But this would not seem to be like a deity, to stand in need of the assistance of others, for that which he wishes to do; for it is a special attribute of God to want nothing; He Himself at least did not in this manner bring forth the seeds from the ground; He only commanded, and they all shot forth.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, that thou mayest learn that it is not the nature of the elements, but His command which effects all things; He both brought into being these very elements which before were not; and without the need of any aid, He brought down the manna for the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it is said, “He gave them bread from heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But why do I say, that in order to the perfection of fruits, the sun requires the aid of other elements for their sustenance; when he himself requires the assistance of many things for his sustenance, and would not himself be sufficient for himself.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For in order that he may proceed on his way, he needs the heaven as a kind of pavement spread out underneath him; and that he may shine, he needs the clearness and rarity of the air; since if even this become unusually dense, he is not able to show his light; and, on the other hand, he requires coolness and moisture, lest his rays should be intolerable to all, and burn up everything.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When, therefore, other elements overrule him, and correct his weakness (overrule as for example, clouds, and walls, and certain other bodies that intercept his light:—or correct his excess, as the dews, and fountains, and cool air), how can such a one be a Deity?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God must be independent, and not stand in need of assistance, be the source of all good things to all, and be hindered by nothing; even as Paul, as well as the prophet Isaiah, saith of God; the latter thus making Him speak in His own Person, “I fill heaven and earth, saith the Lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, “Am I a God nigh at hand, and not a God afar off?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again, David says, “I have said unto the Lord, Thou art my Lord, for Thou hast no need of my good things.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But Paul, demonstrating this independence of help, and shewing that both these things especially belong to God; to stand in need of nothing, and of Himself to supply all things to all; speaks on this wise, “God that made the heaven, and the earth, and the sea, Himself needeth not any thing, giving to all life and all things.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It would indeed be easy for us to take a survey of the other elements, the heaven, the air, the earth, the sea, and to shew the imbecility of these, and how each requires the assistance of his neighbour, and without this assistance, is lost and destroyed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For as it regards the earth, if the fountains fail it, and the moisture infused from the sea and the rivers, it quickly perishes by being parched.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The remaining elements too stand in need of one another, the air of the sun, as well as the sun of the air.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But not to protract this discourse; in what has been said, having given a sufficient supply of reasons to start from for those who are willing to receive them, we shall be content.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if the sun, which is the most surprising part of the whole creation, hath been proved to be so feeble and needy, how much more the other parts of the universe?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What then I have advanced (offering these things for the consideration of the studious), I will myself again shew you in discourse from the Scriptures; and prove, that not only the sun, but also the whole universe is thus corruptible.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since the elements are mutually destructive, and when much cold intervenes, it chastens the force of the sun’s rays; and on the other hand, the heat prevailing, consumes the cold; and since the elements are both the causes and subjects of contrary qualities, and dispositions, in one another; it is very evident that these things offer a proof of great corruptibility; and of the fact, that all these things which are visible, are a corporeal substance.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But since this subject is too lofty for our simplicity, permit me now to lead you to the sweet fountain of the Scriptures, that we may refresh your ears.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For we will not discourse to you of the heaven and the earth separately, but will exhibit the Apostle declaring this very thing to us concerning the whole creation, in these plain terms, that the whole creation is now in bondage to corruption; and why it is thus in bondage, and at what time it shall be delivered from it, and unto what condition it shall be translated.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For after he had said, “The sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory that shall be revealed in us;” he goes on to add; “For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the creature was made subject to vanity, not willingly, but by reason of Him who hath subjected the same in hope.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what he intends is to this effect; “The creature,” he says, “was made corruptible;” for this is implied in the expression, “being made subject to vanity.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it was made corruptible by the command of God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But God so commanded it for the sake of our race; for since it was to nurture a corruptible man, it was necessary itself should also be of the same character; for of course corruptible bodies were not to dwell in an incorruptible creation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But, nevertheless, he tells us, it will not remain so.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “The creature also itself shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption;” and afterwards, for the purpose of shewing when this event shall take place, and through whom, he adds, “Into the glorious liberty of the sons of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when we are raised, his meaning is, and assume incorruptible bodies; then also this body of the heaven, the earth, and the whole creation, shall be incorruptible, and imperishable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When, therefore, thou beholdest the sun arising, admire the Creator; when thou beholdest him hiding himself and disappearing, learn the weakness of his nature, that thou mayest not adore him as a Deity!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God hath not only implanted in the nature of the elements this proof of their weakness, but hath also bidden His servants, that were but men, command them; so that although thou shouldest not know their servitude from their aspect, thou mayest learn, from those who have commanded them, that they are all thy fellow-servants.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore it was, that Joshua, the son of Nave, said, “Let the sun stand still in Gibeon, and the moon over against the valley of Ajalon.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again the prophet Isaiah made the sun to retrace his steps, under the reign of Hezekiah; and Moses gave orders to the air, and the sea, the earth, and the rocks.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Elisha changed the nature of the waters; the Three Children triumphed over the fire.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou seest how God hath provided for us on either hand; leading us by the beauty of the elements to the knowledge of His divinity; and, by their feebleness, not permitting us to lapse into the worship of them.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the sake of all these things then, let us glorify Him, our Guardian; not only by words, but also by deeds; and let us shew forth an excellent conversation, not only in general, but in particular with regard to abstinence from oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not every sin brings the same penalty; but those which are easiest to be amended, bring upon us the greatest punishment: which indeed Solomon intimated, when he said, “It is not wonderful if any one be taken stealing; for he stealeth that he may satisfy his soul that is hungry; but the adulterer, by the lack of understanding, destroyeth his own soul.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what he means is to this effect.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The thief is a grievous offender, but not so grievous a one as the adulterer: for the former, though it be a sorry reason for his conduct, yet at the same time has to plead the necessity arising from indigence; but the latter, when no necessity compels him, by his mere madness rushes into the gulph of iniquity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This also may be said with regard to those who swear.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they have not any pretext to allege, but merely their contempt.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I know, indeed, that I may seem to be too tedious and burdensome; and that I may be thought to give annoyance by continuing this admonition.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But nevertheless, I do not desist, in order that ye may even be shamed by my shamelessness to abstain from the custom of oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if that unmerciful and cruel judge, paying respect to the importunity of the widow, changed his custom, much more will ye do this; and especially when he who is exhorting you, doth it not for himself, but for your salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or rather, indeed, I cannot deny that I do this for myself; for I consider your benefit as my own success.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But I could wish that you, even as I labour, and weary myself for your safety, would in like manner make your own souls a matter of anxiety to yourselves; and then assuredly this work of reformation would be perfected.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what need is there to multiply words?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if there were no hell, neither punishment for the contumacious, nor reward for the obedient; and I had come to you, and asked this in the way of a favour, would ye not have consented? would ye not have granted my petition, when I asked so trifling a favour?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when it is God who asks this favour, and for the sake of yourselves, who are to grant it, and not for Himself, Who is to receive it; who is there so ungracious, who is there so miserable and wretched, that he will not grant this favour to God, when He asks it; and especially when he himself who grants it, is in future to enjoy the benefit of it?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Considering these things then, repeat over to yourselves, when ye depart hence, all that has been said; and correct in every way those who take no heed to it; to the end that we may receive the recompense of other men’s good actions, as well as our own, through the grace and lovingkindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, by Whom, and with Whom be glory to the Father, with the Holy Ghost, for ever and ever.
